text: This is a case of a seventy-two-year-old male who presented with severe anal pain and rectal bleeding. His history revealed that he had a rectal prolapse which could be manually reduced for five years, but it was stuck outside the anus since last week. On rectal examination, there was a prolapsed rectum with ischemia. Since it could not be reduced, emergency perineal rectosigmoidectomy was planned. Perineal approach was preferred due to his comorbidities.

The surgeon makes a circumferential incision on the rectal mucosa one centimeter above the dentate line, carefully cutting through the muscular layer to avoid bleeding or injury. Sutures are used to suspend the anal canal.

The surgeon mobilizes the rectum and returns it to its normal position. The mesorectum is ligated and divided using an energy device at the predetermined resection margin. The colon is cut on one side and secured with a suture to prevent retraction into the abdomen. The resection is completed, and a single-layer anastomosis is performed using separate sutures.
The patient was discharged uneventfully on the fourth postoperative day. There is no evidence of recurrence or any complaints during the follow-up.
